books.title: Paradise Lost
books.author: John Milton
books.summary: Epic poem recounting the biblical fall of man, Satan's rebellion, and Adam and Eve's temptation.
books.historical_context: Written by blind Puritan Milton during English Restoration (1667), epic in blank verse.

books.title: Wuthering Heights
books.author: Emily Brontë
books.summary: Turbulent relationships between families, centered on obsessive love between Heathcliff and Catherine.
books.historical_context: Emily Brontë's only novel (1847), published as 'Ellis Bell,' shocked Victorian readers.

books.title: Jane Eyre
books.author: Charlotte Brontë
books.summary: Orphaned Jane Eyre overcomes hardships to find love with Mr. Rochester while maintaining her independence.
books.historical_context: Published 1847 as 'Currer Bell,' pioneered female Gothic and complex female narrator.

books.title: Pride and Prejudice
books.author: Jane Austen
books.summary: Elizabeth Bennet and wealthy Darcy overcome pride and prejudice to find love while satirizing Regency class and marriage.
books.historical_context: Published anonymously 1813, originally 'First Impressions,' satirizes Regency marriage market.

books.title: Emma
books.author: Jane Austen
books.summary: Spoiled Emma Woodhouse meddles in others' romantic lives while learning lessons about self-knowledge.
books.historical_context: Published in 1815, Emma is Jane Austen's masterpiece of social comedy and literary realism, featuring her most complex heroine in a brilliantly constructed plot that the author said featured 'a heroine whom no one but myself will much like.'

books.title: Sense and Sensibility
books.author: Jane Austen
books.summary: Sisters Elinor (representing sense) and Marianne (representing sensibility) Dashwood face financial hardship and romantic trials, learning to balance rational thought with emotional expression in their pursuit of love and happiness.
books.historical_context: Published in 1811 as Jane Austen's first novel, Sense and Sensibility explores the tension between Neoclassical reason and Romantic emotion through the contrasting Dashwood sisters, establishing Austen's signature social comedy.

books.title: Great Expectations
books.author: Charles Dickens
books.summary: Orphaned Pip rises from blacksmith's apprentice to gentleman, learning genuine worth comes from loyalty, not status.
books.historical_context: Published serially 1860-61, critiques Victorian social climbing and the illusion of gentility.

books.title: David Copperfield
books.author: Charles Dickens
books.summary: David Copperfield's first-person narrative follows his journey from orphaned childhood through cruel stepfather and factory work to eventual success as a novelist, guided by memorable characters like the optimistic Mr. Micawber and treacherous Uriah Heep.
books.historical_context: Published serially 1849-50, Dickens's most autobiographical novel, draws from his childhood.

books.title: Oliver Twist
books.author: Charles Dickens
books.summary: Oliver Twist escapes the brutal workhouse system and falls among thieves in London's criminal underworld, led by the villainous Fagin, until his true parentage is revealed and he finds safety with the benevolent Mr. Brownlow.
books.historical_context: Published serially 1837-39, exposed harsh workhouse conditions and child labor abuses.

books.title: A Tale of Two Cities
books.author: Charles Dickens
books.summary: During French Revolution, Dr. Manette reunites with daughter Lucie while Sydney Carton's sacrifice saves Charles Darnay.
books.historical_context: Published 1859, depicts French Revolution and Reign of Terror, one of best-selling novels.

books.title: Middlemarch
books.author: George Eliot
books.summary: Idealistic Dorothea's marriage troubles parallel Dr. Lydgate's domestic problems in interconnected provincial life.
books.historical_context: Published in installments 1871-72, pioneered psychological realism in Victorian fiction.

books.title: Silas Marner
books.author: George Eliot
books.summary: Betrayed weaver Silas Marner becomes reclusive until golden-haired child Eppie transforms his life.
books.historical_context: Published 1861, explores redemption and transformative power of love over materialism.

books.title: Tess of the d'Urbervilles
books.author: Thomas Hardy
books.summary: Tess's tragic life unfolds from seduction to doomed marriage to murder, challenging Victorian sexual morality.
books.historical_context: Published in 1891 as 'A Pure Woman Faithfully Presented,' Tess of the d'Urbervilles scandalized Victorian society by sympathetically portraying a 'fallen woman,' exposing the sexual double standards that destroyed innocent lives.

books.title: The Mayor of Casterbridge
books.author: Thomas Hardy
books.summary: Michael Henchard's drunken sale of his wife and child at a country fair haunts him as he rises to become mayor of Casterbridge, but his past shame and impulsive nature ultimately destroy his prosperity, relationships, and life.
books.historical_context: Published 1886, explores consequences of past actions in changing Victorian England.

books.title: The Book of the Dead
books.author: Anonymous
books.summary: Collection of spells and instructions for the journey through the afterlife.
books.historical_context: Developed over centuries in ancient Egypt, fundamental to Egyptian religious beliefs.

books.title: The Divine Comedy
books.author: Dante Alighieri
books.summary: Dante journeys through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise guided by Virgil and Beatrice in allegorical quest.
books.historical_context: Written 1308-1320 during Dante's exile, established Italian as literary language.

books.title: The Canterbury Tales
books.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
books.summary: A diverse group of pilgrims traveling to Canterbury Cathedral tell stories that reveal their characters and social positions, creating a vivid cross-section of medieval English society from nobles to peasants.
books.historical_context: Written in Middle English 1387-1400, pioneered realistic character portrayal and satire.

books.title: The Iliad
books.author: Homer
books.summary: Achilles' rage during Trojan War, his conflict with Agamemnon, grief over Patroclus, and killing of Hector.
books.historical_context: Composed in the 8th century BCE, The Iliad is the foundational epic of Western literature, exploring honor, fate, and the human cost of war through the rage of Achilles during the Trojan War's final year.

books.title: The Odyssey
books.author: Homer
books.summary: Odysseus' ten-year journey home from Troy, encountering creatures while Penelope fends off suitors.
books.historical_context: Composed 8th century BCE, sequel to Iliad, established hero's journey archetype.

books.title: The Aeneid
books.author: Virgil
books.summary: The journey of Aeneas from Troy to Italy, where he becomes the ancestor of the Romans.
books.historical_context: Written during Augustus's reign, establishing a mythical connection between Troy and Rome.

books.title: Gulliver's Travels
books.author: Jonathan Swift
books.summary: Lemuel Gulliver's voyages to strange lands satirize human nature and society.
books.historical_context: Published in 1726 by Jonathan Swift, Gulliver's Travels is a savage satire of 18th-century politics, science, and human nature disguised as a fantastical travel narrative, becoming one of the most influential works of English literature.

books.title: The Decameron
books.author: Giovanni Boccaccio
books.summary: A collection of 100 tales told by ten young people sheltering from the Black Death.
books.historical_context: Written during the aftermath of the Black Death, reflecting medieval Italian society and values.

books.title: The Tale of the Heike
books.author: Anonymous
books.summary: The epic account of the struggle between the Taira and Minamoto clans for control of Japan.
books.historical_context: Chronicles the Genpei War (1180-1185), marking the end of the classical period in Japanese history.

books.title: Dead Souls
books.author: Nikolai Gogol
books.summary: Chichikov's scheme to buy deceased serfs' names from landowners, revealing the absurdities of Russian society.
books.historical_context: Written during the reform period of Nicholas I, satirizing Russian serfdom and bureaucracy.

books.title: The Master and Margarita
books.author: Mikhail Bulgakov
books.summary: Satan visits Soviet Moscow, interweaving a love story with a satirical critique of Soviet society.
books.historical_context: Written during Stalin's regime, published posthumously, reflecting the absurdities of Soviet life.

books.title: The Man Without Qualities
books.author: Robert Musil
books.summary: Ulrich's search for meaning in the last days of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.
books.historical_context: Set before World War I, examining the decline of the Habsburg Empire and modern alienation.

books.title: The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy
books.author: Laurence Sterne
books.summary: The rambling, digressive autobiography of Tristram Shandy, who cannot even manage to get himself born.
books.historical_context: Written during the Age of Enlightenment, experimenting with narrative conventions.

books.title: Fathers and Sons
books.author: Ivan Turgenev
books.summary: The conflict between generations and ideologies in 1850s Russia through the story of nihilist Bazarov.
books.historical_context: Written during the period of great reforms in Russia, examining the generational divide.

books.title: The Tin Drum
books.author: Günter Grass
books.summary: Oskar Matzerath, who decides not to grow up, witnesses the rise and fall of Nazi Germany.
books.historical_context: Published in 1959, The Tin Drum launched Gunter Grass to international fame with its surreal, disturbing portrait of Germany from the Nazi era through the postwar economic miracle, told by a boy who refuses to grow.

books.title: The Recognition of Śakuntalā
books.author: Kālidāsa
books.summary: The love story of King Dushyanta and Shakuntala, separated by a curse and reunited through a ring.
books.historical_context: Written during India's classical period, exemplifying Sanskrit dramatic traditions.

books.title: The Dream of the Red Chamber
books.author: Cao Xueqin
books.summary: The rise and fall of the Jia family and the love story between Jia Baoyu and Lin Daiyu.
books.historical_context: Written during the Qing Dynasty in the mid-18th century, The Dream of the Red Chamber depicts the decline of a noble Chinese family with unprecedented psychological depth, considered the greatest of China's Four Classic Novels.

books.title: Season of Migration to the North
books.author: Tayeb Salih
books.summary: A Sudanese man's experiences in England and his return home, exploring colonialism and identity.
books.historical_context: Written post-independence, examining the complex relationship between Africa and Europe.

books.title: The Golden Notebook
books.author: Doris Lessing
books.summary: Writer Anna Wulf's attempt to integrate different aspects of her life through four notebooks.
books.historical_context: Written during the Cold War, exploring feminism, communism, and mental breakdown.

books.title: Death Comes for the Archbishop
books.author: Willa Cather
books.summary: The life of a French Catholic priest building a diocese in the American Southwest.
books.historical_context: Set in the mid-19th century, depicting the American frontier and cultural encounters.

books.title: The Plague
books.author: Albert Camus
books.summary: A city's response to a deadly plague, examining human resilience and solidarity.
books.historical_context: Written after World War II, using plague as an allegory for the Nazi occupation.

books.title: The Master of Go
books.author: Yasunari Kawabata
books.summary: Reporter surrogate chronicles the 1938 Go match where aging Master Shusai faces modern challenger Otake, symbolizing shifting Japan.
books.historical_context: Published in 1954, The Master of Go is Yasunari Kawabata's fictionalized account of a historic 1938 Go match, reflecting Japan's cultural traditions yielding to modernity in the postwar era, foreshadowing themes that won him the 1968 Nobel Prize.

books.title: Austerlitz
books.author: W.G. Sebald
books.summary: Architectural historian Jacques Austerlitz uncovers his Kindertransport past and lost parents through wandering European stations.
books.historical_context: Sebald's 2001 novel blended essay and fiction, meditating on Holocaust memory at the millennium.

books.title: The Leopard
books.author: Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa
books.summary: Prince Fabrizio of Salina witnesses Sicily's aristocracy fade during Italy's Risorgimento, accepting change to preserve appearance.
books.historical_context: Written 1958 from memoirs of decline, Lampedusa portrayed 1860s unification upheaval.

books.title: The Tale of Kieu
books.author: Nguyễn Du
books.summary: Virtuous Kieu sells herself into bondage to save her family, enduring exile before karmic reunion with her beloved Kim.
books.historical_context: Nguyễn Du's 1820 verse novel adapted a Chinese tale to critique Confucian morality in Nguyen‑dynasty Vietnam.

books.title: Pedro Páramo
books.author: Juan Rulfo
books.summary: Juan Preciado arrives in Comala to meet his father but finds a ghost town haunted by the patriarch's sins.
books.historical_context: Rulfo's 1955 work pioneered Latin American surrealism amid Mexico's post‑Revolution disillusionment.

books.title: The Complete Stories of Flannery O'Connor
books.author: Flannery O'Connor
books.summary: Grotesque Southerners confront grace, violence and revelation in short tales blending dark humor with Catholic theology.
books.historical_context: Published 1971 after O'Connor's death, the collection spanned 1940s‑60s Georgia amid Civil Rights tensions.

books.title: The Book of Disquiet
books.author: Fernando Pessoa
books.summary: Lisbon clerk Bernardo Soares records fragmentary reflections on alienation, dreams and the futility of identity.
books.historical_context: Texts written by Pessoa in the 1910s-1930s; posthumously assembled and published in 1982.

books.title: Blood Meridian
books.author: Cormac McCarthy
books.summary: A nameless Kid joins Glanton's scalp‑hunting gang, riding a psychedelic bloodbath across the 1840s borderlands under Judge Holden's nihilistic gaze.
books.historical_context: McCarthy's 1985 novel re‑examined Manifest Destiny through gothic violence in the American Southwest.

books.title: The Mahabharata
books.author: Vyasa
books.summary: Rival cousins battle in the Kurukshetra War while divine sermons like the Bhagavad Gita explore duty, karma and cosmic order.
books.historical_context: Compiled between 400 BCE and 400 CE, this Sanskrit epic shaped Hindu philosophy and subcontinental identity.

books.title: The Possessed
books.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
books.summary: Revolutionary agitators descend on a provincial Russian town, exploiting nihilism and provoking murder in ideological chaos.
books.historical_context: Dostoevsky's 1872 novel criticized radical politics emerging in Tsarist Russia after the 1860s reforms.

books.title: The Makioka Sisters
books.author: Junichiro Tanizaki
books.summary: Four Osaka sisters navigate arranged marriages and Western influence as their aristocratic family declines on the eve of WWII.
books.historical_context: Tanizaki serialized the novel 1943‑48, recording Japan's shift from tradition to modernity before wartime defeat.

books.title: If on a winter's night a traveler
books.author: Italo Calvino
books.summary: A Reader tries to finish a novel that keeps restarting in different genres, collapsing boundaries between book and audience.
books.historical_context: Calvino's 1979 postmodern experiment reflected late‑20th‑century skepticism about narrative authority.

books.title: The Great Gatsby
books.author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
books.summary: Mysterious millionaire Jay Gatsby's pursuit of lost love Daisy Buchanan exposes the corruption and disillusionment beneath the glittering surface of the Jazz Age, narrated by neighbor Nick Carraway.
books.historical_context: Published 1925, captures Roaring Twenties excess and critiques the American Dream.

books.title: Ulysses
books.author: James Joyce
books.summary: Leopold Bloom's ordinary day in Dublin parallels Homer's Odyssey through stream-of-consciousness narrative.
books.historical_context: Published in 1922, Ulysses is James Joyce's modernist masterpiece following Leopold Bloom through a single day in Dublin, initially banned for obscenity but now recognized as perhaps the greatest novel of the 20th century.

books.title: In Search of Lost Time
books.author: Marcel Proust
books.summary: Marcel reconstructs loves, art and decadence in fin-de-siècle France triggered by madeleine's taste.
books.historical_context: Proust's 1913‑27 masterpiece chronicled Belle Époque society dissolving before World War I.

books.title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
books.author: Gabriel García Márquez
books.summary: The multi-generational saga of the Buendía family in the mythical town of Macondo, blending magical realism with Latin American history, exploring themes of solitude, fate, and the cyclical nature of history.
books.historical_context: Published in 1967, One Hundred Years of Solitude defined magical realism and earned Gabriel Garcia Marquez the Nobel Prize, chronicling seven generations of the Buendia family in the mythical town of Macondo.

books.title: The Catcher in the Rye
books.author: J.D. Salinger
books.summary: Sixteen-year-old Holden Caulfield wanders New York City after being expelled from prep school, struggling with depression, alienation, and his inability to connect with what he sees as an adult world full of "phonies."
books.historical_context: Published 1951, captured post-war teenage disillusionment, often banned in schools.

books.title: Nineteen Eighty-Four
books.author: George Orwell
books.summary: Winston Smith secretly rebels in a surveillance state where Big Brother rewrites history and even thought is a crime.
books.historical_context: Orwell's 1949 novel reflected post‑war anxieties about Stalinism, propaganda and emerging Cold War authoritarianism.

books.title: Lolita
books.author: Vladimir Nabokov
books.summary: Humbert Humbert's obsession with twelve-year-old Dolores Haze.
books.historical_context: Written during the 1950s, exploring themes of obsession, manipulation, and moral corruption.

books.title: The Lord of the Rings
books.author: J.R.R. Tolkien
books.summary: Hobbit Frodo must destroy the One Ring to defeat Sauron, accompanied by fellowship through Middle-earth.
books.historical_context: Published 1954-1955, established modern fantasy literature from Tolkien's medieval expertise.

books.title: Moby-Dick
books.author: Herman Melville
books.summary: Captain Ahab's obsessive quest for revenge against the white whale that took his leg drives the crew of the Pequod into dangerous waters, narrated by survivor Ishmael in a philosophical meditation on fate, nature, and obsession.
books.historical_context: Published 1851, initially failed commercially but now considered American masterpiece.

books.title: To Kill a Mockingbird
books.author: Harper Lee
books.summary: Young Scout Finch learns about racial injustice and moral courage when her father Atticus defends a black man falsely accused of rape in Depression-era Alabama, observing the destructive nature of prejudice and hatred.
books.historical_context: Published 1960, won Pulitzer Prize, became classic on racial injustice and moral education.

books.title: The Tale of Genji
books.author: Murasaki Shikibu
books.summary: The life and romantic pursuits of Hikaru Genji, the emperor's son, in the imperial court.
books.historical_context: Written during the height of the Heian period, depicting aristocratic culture and customs.

books.title: The Thousand and One Nights
books.author: Various Authors
books.summary: A collection of Middle Eastern folk tales framed by Scheherazade's nightly storytelling.
books.historical_context: Compiled over centuries during the Islamic Golden Age (8th-14th centuries), The Thousand and One Nights reflects the cosmopolitan culture of medieval Baghdad and Cairo, weaving together Persian, Indian, and Arabic storytelling traditions.

books.title: The Epic of Gilgamesh
books.author: Anonymous
books.summary: The adventures of King Gilgamesh and his friend Enkidu, exploring themes of friendship and mortality.
books.historical_context: One of the earliest surviving works of literature, from ancient Sumerian civilization.

books.title: Beloved
books.author: Toni Morrison
books.summary: A former slave is haunted by the ghost of her daughter in post-Civil War Ohio.
books.historical_context: Set in the aftermath of the Civil War, examining the psychological impact of slavery.

books.title: The Sound and the Fury
books.author: William Faulkner
books.summary: The decline of the Compson family told through multiple perspectives.
books.historical_context: Published in 1929, The Sound and the Fury chronicles the decline of the Compson family in the American South, with Faulkner pioneering stream-of-consciousness narration to depict the collapse of Southern aristocratic values.

books.title: The Stranger
books.author: Albert Camus
books.summary: Meursault's emotional detachment leads to murder and a philosophical examination of existence.
books.historical_context: Published in 1942 during French colonial rule in Algeria, The Stranger launched existentialist literature with its indifferent narrator Meursault, whose emotional detachment from his mother's death and a killing expose the absurdity of human conventions.

books.title: The Metamorphosis
books.author: Franz Kafka
books.summary: Traveling salesman Gregor Samsa awakens to find himself transformed into a giant insect, becoming a burden to his family and exploring themes of alienation, guilt, and the dehumanizing effects of modern life.
books.historical_context: Published 1915, Kafka's surreal masterpiece epitomizes modernism and existential anxiety.

books.title: Things Fall Apart
books.author: Chinua Achebe
books.summary: The life of Okonkwo and his village's encounter with British colonialism.
books.historical_context: Published in 1958, Things Fall Apart was set in pre-colonial and colonial Nigeria, examining the devastating cultural clash between Igbo society and European imperialism, becoming the most widely read African novel in history.

books.title: Beowulf
books.author: Anonymous
books.summary: An Old English epic poem featuring the hero Beowulf, who battles the monster Grendel, Grendel's mother, and a dragon.
books.historical_context: Likely composed between the 8th and 11th centuries, it is one of the most important works of Anglo-Saxon literature.

books.title: Shahnameh
books.author: Ferdowsi
books.summary: Epic poem narrating the legendary and historical past of Persia, from the creation of the world until the Arab conquest.
books.historical_context: Composed over 30 years, preserving Persian cultural identity after the Islamic conquest.

books.title: Don Quixote
books.author: Miguel de Cervantes
books.summary: Aging gentleman Alonso Quixano loses his sanity from reading chivalric romances and becomes the knight-errant Don Quixote, accompanied by loyal squire Sancho Panza on delusional but idealistic adventures across Spain.
books.historical_context: Published 1605-1615, often considered first modern novel, satirizes chivalric romance.

books.title: War and Peace
books.author: Leo Tolstoy
books.summary: Russian aristocratic families during Napoleonic era, following Pierre, Natasha, and Andrei through love and war.
books.historical_context: Published serially 1865-1869, Tolstoy's epic combines character development with historical narrative.

books.title: Crime and Punishment
books.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
books.summary: Impoverished student Raskolnikov murders an elderly pawnbroker to prove his superiority theory but struggles with guilt and paranoia until love and confession lead to his spiritual redemption through Siberian exile.
books.historical_context: Published 1866, psychological thriller exploring crime's aftermath, guilt, and redemption.

books.title: Anna Karenina
books.author: Leo Tolstoy
books.summary: Anna's affair with Vronsky destroys her life, paralleled by Levin's spiritual journey and marriage.
books.historical_context: Published serially 1875-1877, examines individual desire vs. social conventions in Russia.

books.title: The Brothers Karamazov
books.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
books.summary: The murder of patriarch Fyodor Karamazov exposes the philosophical and moral conflicts between his four sons - sensualist Dmitri, intellectual Ivan, spiritual Alyosha, and illegitimate Smerdyakov - in a exploration of faith, reason, and free will.
books.historical_context: Published serially 1879-1880, Dostoevsky's final novel explores faith and rationalism.

books.title: Madame Bovary
books.author: Gustave Flaubert
books.summary: Emma Bovary's romantic fantasies and material desires lead her into adultery and debt, culminating in suicide as she struggles against the mundane reality of provincial bourgeois life in 19th-century France.
books.historical_context: Published 1857, prosecuted for immorality, established modern novel form through realism.

books.title: The Trial
books.author: Franz Kafka
books.summary: Josef K. is arrested and prosecuted by a mysterious bureaucratic court system for an unknown crime.
books.historical_context: Kafka's surreal exploration of bureaucracy, guilt, and alienation in modern society.

books.title: To the Lighthouse
books.author: Virginia Woolf
books.summary: The Ramsay family's visits to their summer home in the Hebrides are interrupted by World War I, exploring the passage of time, death, and the complexity of human relationships through stream-of-consciousness narrative.
books.historical_context: Published 1927, Woolf's modernist masterpiece pioneered stream-of-consciousness and feminism.

books.title: The Magic Mountain
books.author: Thomas Mann
books.summary: Hans Castorp's stay at a tuberculosis sanatorium becomes a philosophical education about European civilization.
books.historical_context: Published in 1924, The Magic Mountain is Thomas Mann's sweeping meditation on European intellectual culture, disease, and death set in a Swiss sanatorium, prophetically capturing the ideological tensions that would lead to two world wars.

books.title: Mrs. Dalloway
books.author: Virginia Woolf
books.summary: A single day in post-WWI London follows society hostess Clarissa Dalloway preparing for a party while shell-shocked veteran Septimus Warren Smith struggles with trauma, their stories converging in themes of time and consciousness.
books.historical_context: Published 1925, used stream-of-consciousness to explore WWI's psychological aftermath.

books.title: Catch-22
books.author: Joseph Heller
books.summary: Captain Yossarian navigates the absurd bureaucracy and catch-22 logic of military life during WWII.
books.historical_context: Heller's satirical anti-war novel critiquing military bureaucracy and the absurdity of war.

books.title: Invisible Man
books.author: Ralph Ellison
books.summary: An unnamed Black narrator's journey from the South to Harlem explores racism and identity in America.
books.historical_context: Ellison's groundbreaking novel examining African American experience and social invisibility.

books.title: Les Misérables
books.author: Victor Hugo
books.summary: Ex-convict Jean Valjean seeks redemption while pursued by inspector Javert amid French social upheaval.
books.historical_context: Published 1862, Hugo's epic exposes French social injustices while advocating reform.

books.title: On the Road
books.author: Jack Kerouac
books.summary: Sal Paradise and Dean Moriarty's cross-country travels capture Beat Generation spirit and American freedom.
books.historical_context: Kerouac's spontaneous prose chronicling 1950s counterculture and the search for meaning.

books.title: Journey to the End of the Night
books.author: Louis-Ferdinand Céline
books.summary: Bardamu's cynical journey through WWI, colonial Africa, and urban America reveals humanity's darkness.
books.historical_context: Céline's controversial novel revolutionizing French literature with colloquial language and nihilistic vision.

books.title: The Red and the Black
books.author: Stendhal
books.summary: Julien Sorel's ambitious rise through French society ends in scandal and execution.
books.historical_context: Stendhal's psychological realism exploring ambition, love, and social climbing in post-Revolutionary France.

books.title: Fictions
books.author: Jorge Luis Borges
books.summary: Borges' labyrinthine short stories explore infinity, identity, and the nature of reality through philosophical fiction.
books.historical_context: Written during WWII, these stories established magical realism and postmodern literature.

books.title: Brave New World
books.author: Aldous Huxley
books.summary: In genetically engineered future, Bernard Marx and John 'the Savage' challenge the dehumanizing system.
books.historical_context: Published in 1932, Brave New World warned against the dangers of genetic engineering, conditioning, and consumer culture, presenting a dystopia of pleasure rather than pain that proved prophetically relevant to modern technological society.

books.title: Animal Farm
books.author: George Orwell
books.summary: Farm animals overthrow humans but pigs become corrupt dictators in allegory of Russian Revolution.
books.historical_context: Published 1945, political allegory critiquing Soviet communism through farmyard fable.

books.title: The Old Man and the Sea
books.author: Ernest Hemingway
books.summary: An aging fisherman struggles with a giant marlin in the Gulf Stream, testing his endurance and spirit.
books.historical_context: Hemingway's novella about perseverance earned him the Nobel Prize in Literature.

books.title: The Diary of a Young Girl
books.author: Anne Frank
books.summary: Anne Frank's diary chronicles her family's hiding during the Nazi occupation of the Netherlands.
books.historical_context: Published posthumously, Anne's diary became a powerful testament to the Holocaust.

books.title: Little Women
books.author: Louisa May Alcott
books.summary: The March sisters - Jo, Meg, Beth, and Amy - navigate growing up during the American Civil War.
books.historical_context: Alcott's novel promoted female independence and domesticity during the Victorian era.

books.title: Hamlet
books.author: William Shakespeare
books.summary: Prince Hamlet seeks revenge against uncle Claudius for murdering his father and marrying his mother.
books.historical_context: Written 1600-1601, Shakespeare's most performed play, explores revenge and mortality.

books.title: Their Eyes Were Watching God
books.author: Zora Neale Hurston
books.summary: Janie Crawford's journey through three marriages explores African American womanhood and self-discovery.
books.historical_context: Hurston's novel was rediscovered during the civil rights movement as a masterpiece of African American literature.

books.title: Rebecca
books.author: Daphne du Maurier
books.summary: A young woman marries a widower and struggles with the memory of his deceased first wife, Rebecca.
books.historical_context: Du Maurier's psychological thriller influenced Gothic romance and suspense fiction.

books.title: Gone with the Wind
books.author: Margaret Mitchell
books.summary: Scarlett O'Hara's survival during the American Civil War and Reconstruction era in the antebellum South.
books.historical_context: Published in 1936, Gone with the Wind became an instant bestseller and won the Pulitzer Prize, with Margaret Mitchell's epic portrayal of the Civil War South becoming one of the most widely read American novels despite its romanticized view of slavery.

books.title: Slaughterhouse-Five
books.author: Kurt Vonnegut
books.summary: Billy Pilgrim becomes unstuck in time, experiencing the Dresden bombing and alien abduction.
books.historical_context: Vonnegut's anti-war novel draws from his experience as a POW during the Dresden bombing.

books.title: A Passage to India
books.author: E.M. Forster
books.summary: Dr. Aziz's friendship with English visitors explores cultural misunderstanding in colonial India.
books.historical_context: Forster's novel examines British-Indian relations during the independence movement.

books.title: Lord of the Flies
books.author: William Golding
books.summary: British schoolboys stranded on an island descend into savagery and tribal warfare.
books.historical_context: Published in 1954, Lord of the Flies is William Golding's dark allegory of civilization's fragility, in which British schoolboys stranded on an island descend into savagery, reflecting post-WWII anxieties about human nature.

books.title: Candide
books.author: Voltaire
books.summary: Candide's journey through disasters and adventures satirizes philosophical optimism.
books.historical_context: Published in 1759, Candide is Voltaire's biting satire that critiques Leibnizian optimism and European society through its hapless hero's misadventures across the globe, becoming the quintessential Enlightenment novel.

books.title: The Handmaid's Tale
books.author: Margaret Atwood
books.summary: Offred navigates a totalitarian theocracy where women are subjugated for reproductive purposes.
books.historical_context: Atwood's dystopia warns against fundamentalism and the erosion of women's rights.

books.title: Leaves of Grass
books.author: Walt Whitman
books.summary: Whitman's poetry collection celebrates American democracy, nature, and the human spirit.
books.historical_context: First published in 1855 and continuously revised until Whitman's death, Leaves of Grass is the revolutionary free verse poetry collection that defined American literary identity, celebrating democracy, the body, and the individual.

books.title: Robinson Crusoe
books.author: Daniel Defoe
books.summary: Shipwrecked Robinson Crusoe survives alone on a deserted island for decades, learning self-reliance and resourcefulness.
books.historical_context: Defoe's novel established the castaway genre and explored themes of civilization and colonialism.

books.title: The Bell Jar
books.author: Sylvia Plath
books.summary: Esther Greenwood's descent into mental illness mirrors Plath's own struggles with depression and suicide.
books.historical_context: Published posthumously, Plath's semi-autobiographical novel destigmatized mental health discussions.

books.title: Waiting for Godot
books.author: Samuel Beckett
books.summary: Vladimir and Estragon wait endlessly for the mysterious Godot who never arrives.
books.historical_context: Beckett's absurdist play revolutionized theater and captured post-war existential anxiety.

books.title: Native Son
books.author: Richard Wright
books.summary: Bigger Thomas's violent actions expose the systemic racism and poverty of 1930s Chicago.
books.historical_context: Wright's groundbreaking novel confronted American racism with unprecedented directness.

books.title: Vanity Fair
books.author: William Makepeace Thackeray
books.summary: Becky Sharp's social climbing and Amelia Sedley's goodness contrast in Regency society.
books.historical_context: Thackeray's satire critiqued Victorian society and the pursuit of wealth and status.

books.title: The Color Purple
books.author: Alice Walker
books.summary: Celie's letters chronicle her journey from abuse to empowerment in the Jim Crow South.
books.historical_context: Published in 1982 and winning the Pulitzer Prize, The Color Purple is Alice Walker's epistolary novel addressing the intersectional oppression of African American women in the rural South, transforming trauma into a story of spiritual liberation.

books.title: Walden
books.author: Henry David Thoreau
books.summary: Thoreau's two-year experiment in simple living explores nature, solitude, and self-reliance.
books.historical_context: Thoreau's transcendentalist philosophy influenced environmental and civil rights movements.

books.title: Treasure Island
books.author: Robert Louis Stevenson
books.summary: Young Jim Hawkins joins a treasure hunt that leads to pirate adventures and betrayal.
books.historical_context: Stevenson's adventure novel defined the pirate genre and influenced countless adaptations.

books.title: The Long Goodbye
books.author: Raymond Chandler
books.summary: Detective Philip Marlowe investigates the suicide of Terry Lennox and uncovers corruption.
books.historical_context: Chandler's noir masterpiece elevated the detective genre with literary sophistication.

books.title: A Sentimental Education
books.author: Gustave Flaubert
books.summary: Idealistic law student Frédéric Moreau nurses an obsessive love for the married Madame Arnoux while drifting through the 1848 Paris revolution, charting ambition and disappointment.
books.historical_context: Flaubert’s 1869 novel dissected the moral vacuity of France’s July Monarchy and Second Empire generations.

books.title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
books.author: Douglas Adams
books.summary: Arthur Dent hitchhikes through space with alien Ford Prefect after Earth's demolition.
books.historical_context: Adapted from Adams’s 1978 BBC radio comedy, the 1979 novel skewered late‑20th‑century British life and sci‑fi clichés.

books.title: The Three Musketeers
books.author: Alexandre Dumas
books.summary: Young d'Artagnan joins musketeers to thwart Cardinal Richelieu's intrigues in 17th-century France.
books.historical_context: First serialized in 1844, Dumas’s swashbuckler fed Romantic nostalgia during France’s July Monarchy.

books.title: Giovanni's Room
books.author: James Baldwin
books.summary: American drifter David grapples with sexual identity during a doomed affair with Italian bartender Giovanni in the bohemian haunts of 1950s Paris.
books.historical_context: Baldwin’s 1956 novel challenged taboos around same‑sex desire and racial outsiderhood in mid‑century literature.

books.title: Howards End
books.author: E. M. Forster
books.summary: The idealistic Schlegel sisters become entwined with the pragmatic Wilcox family and impoverished clerk Leonard Bast, questioning class and connection over a beloved country house.
books.historical_context: Forster’s 1910 work exposed Edwardian England’s shifting social order on the eve of World War I.

books.title: And Then There Were None
books.author: Agatha Christie
books.summary: Ten guests lured to a remote island are murdered one by one according to a grim nursery rhyme, as past crimes surface.
books.historical_context: Christie’s 1939 mystery innovated the closed‑circle thriller against a backdrop of pre‑war anxiety.

books.title: A Room of One's Own
books.author: Virginia Woolf
books.summary: Expanding a Cambridge lecture, Woolf argues that women need financial independence and private space to create literature and challenge patriarchy.
books.historical_context: Published in 1929, the essay became a cornerstone of feminist literary criticism between the wars.

books.title: Fahrenheit 451
books.author: Ray Bradbury
books.summary: Fireman Guy Montag burns books for a living until forbidden literature sparks rebellion against his screen‑obsessed, authoritarian society.
books.historical_context: Bradbury’s 1953 dystopia critiqued McCarthy‑era censorship and mass‑media conformity in Cold War America.

books.title: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
books.author: Ken Kesey
books.summary: Rebellious Randle McMurphy challenges the authoritarian Nurse Ratched in a psychiatric hospital, inspiring fellow patients to assert their individuality before his tragic lobotomy and death.
books.historical_context: Published 1962, critiqued institutional authority during 1960s counterculture movement.

books.title: Tender Is the Night
books.author: F. Scott Fitzgerald

location.name: France, French riviera, Paris, Switzerland, United States

books.summary: Glamorous psychiatrist Dick Diver and his wealthy, fragile wife Nicole spiral into dissolution amid Riviera decadence and shifting power dynamics.
books.historical_context: Fitzgerald’s 1934 novel mirrored Jazz Age expatriates and the author’s struggle with Zelda’s mental illness.

books.title: The Prince
books.author: Niccolo Machiavelli
books.summary: Machiavelli offers blunt counsel on how rulers should balance virtue and ruthlessness to secure and maintain political power.
books.historical_context: Written in 1513 after the Medici coup and published posthumously in 1532, the treatise codified Renaissance realpolitik.

books.title: Harry Potter And The Philosopher's Stone
books.author: J. K. Rowling
books.summary: Orphan Harry discovers he is a wizard, attends Hogwarts, and faces dark sorcerer Voldemort while forging friendships with Ron and Hermione.
books.historical_context: Rowling’s 1997 debut revitalized children’s fantasy and launched a global multimedia phenomenon.

books.title: Steppenwolf
books.author: Hermann Hesse
books.summary: Alienated intellectual Harry Haller, torn between bourgeois respectability and wolfish instinct, seeks transcendence through the surreal Magic Theatre.
books.historical_context: Hesse’s 1927 novel captured Weimar‑era existential angst and later influenced 1960s counterculture.

books.title: Love in the Time of Cholera
books.author: Gabriel García Márquez
books.summary: Florentino Ariza endures half a century to rekindle love with Fermina Daza after her husband’s death amid Caribbean cholera outbreaks.
books.historical_context: García Márquez’s 1985 novel blended magical realism with Colombian history, examining enduring passion and aging.

books.title: The Complete Sherlock Holmes
books.author: Arthur Conan Doyle
books.summary: Doyle’s four novels and 56 stories follow detective Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson as they apply keen deduction to Victorian mysteries.
books.historical_context: Written 1887‑1927, the canon established modern detective fiction and popularized forensic methodology.

books.title: Confessions of Zeno
books.author: Italo Svevo
books.summary: Self‑deluding Trieste businessman Zeno Cosini recounts his life and addictions under psychoanalysis, revealing the comic elasticity of truth.
books.historical_context: Svevo’s 1923 modernist novel echoed Freud’s influence and Austro‑Italian identity crises on the eve of WWI.

books.title: Light in August
books.author: William Faulkner
books.summary: Orphan Joe Christmas’s violent odyssey intersects with wanderer Lena Grove in Yoknapatawpha County, probing race, religion and identity.
books.historical_context: Faulkner’s 1932 work exposed Deep South racial tensions during the Great Depression.

books.title: Lord Jim
books.author: Joseph Conrad
books.summary: After abandoning ship in a moment of cowardice, seaman Jim searches for redemption in the remote Malay settlement of Patusan.
books.historical_context: Conrad’s 1900 novel examined colonial guilt and romantic idealism within the British maritime empire.

books.title: The Hobbit
books.author: J. R. R. Tolkien
books.summary: Comfort‑loving Bilbo Baggins joins thirteen dwarves on a quest to reclaim treasure from the dragon Smaug, discovering courage and cunning.
books.historical_context: Published in 1937, Tolkien’s children’s fantasy drew on Norse mythology and prefigured The Lord of the Rings.

books.title: Rabbit, Run
books.author: John Updike

location.name: Mount Judge, Pennsylvania, Suburbia, United States

books.summary: Discontented former basketball star Harry “Rabbit” Angstrom flees his pregnant wife in 1959 Pennsylvania, chasing freedom yet finding moral dead ends.
books.historical_context: Updike’s 1960 novel inaugurated a tetralogy chronicling post‑war American suburbia and the search for meaning.

books.title: Man's Fate
books.author: Andre Malraux
books.summary: Chinese communists and nationalists collide during the failed 1927 Shanghai uprising, exploring existential commitment and political sacrifice.
books.historical_context: Malraux’s 1933 Prix Goncourt‑winning novel drew on his revolutionary experiences amid Asia’s turbulent interwar era.

books.title: The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie
books.author: Muriel Spark
books.summary: Charismatic Edinburgh teacher Jean Brodie molds her ‘set’ of schoolgirls with unorthodox lessons—until loyalty and fascist sympathies undo her.
books.historical_context: Spark’s 1961 novella satirized 1930s Scottish conservatism and post‑war cults of personality.

books.title: Confessions
books.author: Augustine
books.summary: Bishop Augustine recounts his youth of ambition and sensuality, his conversion to Christianity, and meditations on sin and grace.
books.historical_context: Written c. 397 CE in Latin North Africa, it pioneered introspective autobiography and shaped medieval Christian theology.

books.title: White Teeth
books.author: Zadie Smith
books.summary: Interlinked London families—Jamaican, Bangladeshi and white working‑class—navigate immigration, identity and genetic science from WWII to 1992.
books.historical_context: Smith’s 2000 debut captured multicultural Britain at the millennium after post‑colonial migration.

books.title: The Spy Who Came in From the Cold
books.author: John le Carré

location.name: Berlin, Germany, Great Britain, United Kingdom

books.summary: A weary British agent undertakes one last deception in East Berlin, blurring morality amid Cold War espionage.
books.historical_context: Le Carré’s 1963 novel injected bleak realism into spy fiction during heightened East‑West tensions.

books.title: Metamorphoses
books.author: Ovid
books.summary: Ovid weaves 250 myths of gods and mortals linked by transformative change, from creation to Julius Caesar’s deification.
books.historical_context: Completed in 8 CE, the Latin epic bridged Augustan Rome’s literary golden age and influenced Western art for millennia.

books.title: A Good Man Is Hard to Find
books.author: Flannery O'Connor
books.summary: In Flannery O’Connor’s title story and others, grotesque Southerners confront violent moments of grace and revelation.
books.historical_context: Published in 1955, the collection blended Catholic theology with Jim Crow Georgia’s moral contradictions.

books.title: Winnie the Pooh
books.author: A. A. Milne
books.summary: The gentle adventures of Pooh Bear and friends in Hundred Acre Wood celebrate imagination, friendship and childlike wonder.
books.historical_context: Milne’s 1926 classic, inspired by his son’s toys, became a cornerstone of 20th‑century children’s literature.

books.title: The Flowers of Evil
books.author: Charles Baudelaire
books.summary: Baudelaire’s poems juxtapose beauty and decay, erotic desire and spiritual agony amid modern Parisian life.
books.historical_context: First published in 1857 and censored for obscenity, it heralded French Symbolism and urban modernity.

books.title: Brighton Rock
books.author: Graham Greene
books.summary: Teenage gangster Pinkie Brown marries naïve waitress Rose to silence her, while relentless Ida Arnold pursues justice along Brighton’s seedy pier.
books.historical_context: Greene’s 1938 ‘Catholic thriller’ examined sin and damnation beneath seaside holiday culture before WWII.

books.title: The Corrections
books.author: Jonathan Franzen

location.name: Midwest, New York, New York City, Pennsylvania, United States

books.summary: Midwestern matriarch Enid Lambert schemes to reunite her adult children for one last Christmas, exposing familial dysfunction and late‑90s anxieties.
books.historical_context: Franzen’s 2001 novel chronicled pre‑dot‑com‑crash America and won the National Book Award.

books.title: On the Origin of Species
books.author: Charles Darwin
books.summary: Darwin argues that natural selection drives species evolution, marshaling evidence from geology, breeding and biogeography.
books.historical_context: Published in 1859, the treatise revolutionized biology and challenged Victorian religious orthodoxy.

books.title: Of Mice and Men
books.author: John Steinbeck
books.summary: Itinerant ranch hands George and mentally limited Lennie chase a dream farm but face tragic consequences in Depression‑era California.
books.historical_context: Steinbeck’s 1937 novella highlighted migrant labor struggles during America’s Dust Bowl years.

books.title: Fairy Tales and Stories
books.author: Hans Christian Andersen
books.summary: Andersen’s collection ranges from ‘The Little Mermaid’ to ‘The Snow Queen,’ blending whimsy with poignant moral lessons.
books.historical_context: Written 1835‑72, these Danish tales transcended folklore to craft modern literary fairy stories.

books.title: A Wrinkle In Time
books.author: Madeleine L'Engle
books.summary: Meg Murry and companions traverse space-time via tesseract to rescue her scientist father.
books.historical_context: L’Engle’s 1962 Newbery winner merged science fiction with spiritual themes amid early‑Cold‑War optimism.

books.title: The End of the Affair
books.author: Graham Greene
books.summary: During post‑Blitz London, novelist Maurice Bendrix obsessively investigates why his lover Sarah suddenly ended their adulterous affair.
books.historical_context: Greene’s 1951 novel explored faith, jealousy and wartime trauma in ration‑era Britain.

books.title: Molloy
books.author: Samuel Beckett
books.summary: In fragmented monologues, bedridden Molloy and detective Moran recount absurd quests, eroding narrative certainty and identity.
books.historical_context: Beckett’s 1951 French‑language novel inaugurated his existential trilogy during post‑war avant‑garde literature.

books.title: The Remains of the Day
books.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
books.summary: English butler Stevens recalls a lifetime of duty at Darlington Hall, confronting missed love and complicity with appeasement.
books.historical_context: Ishiguro’s 1989 Booker winner reflected Thatcher‑era reevaluations of Britain’s fading imperial past.

books.title: Portnoy's Complaint
books.author: Philip Roth

location.name: New Jersey, New York, Newark, United States

books.summary: Through psychoanalytic confession, Alexander Portnoy hilariously exposes sexual obsessions, guilt and Jewish family neuroses.
books.historical_context: Roth’s 1969 novel shocked American mores amid the sexual revolution and New York Jewish identity.

books.title: An American Tragedy
books.author: Theodore Dreiser
books.summary: Ambitious factory worker Clyde Griffiths seduces two women; when pregnancy threatens his rise, he plots murder on a lonely Adirondack lake, exposing class and justice in 1920s America.
books.historical_context: Dreiser’s 1925 naturalist novel, inspired by a 1906 murder case, critiqued the corrosive pursuit of the American Dream during the Jazz Age.

books.title: The Good Soldier Svejk
books.author: Jaroslav Hašek
books.summary: Guileless Czech Josef Švejk survives absurd orders and red‑tape while serving the Austro‑Hungarian army in World War I, poking fun at militarism.
books.historical_context: Hašek serialized the satire 1921‑23 in newly formed Czechoslovakia, channeling anarchist and anti‑war sentiment.

books.title: Watership Down
books.author: Richard Adams
books.summary: Led by Hazel and prophetic Fiver, a band of rabbits flee warren doom, founding a new colony after battles with predators and authoritarian Efrafa.
books.historical_context: Adams’s 1972 ecological epic blended animal fantasy with leadership allegory amid rising environmental awareness.

books.title: The Counterfeiters
books.author: André Gide
books.summary: Intersecting Parisian youths and writers test boundaries of sexuality, art and truth while novelist Édouard drafts a book mirroring their lives.
books.historical_context: Gide’s 1925 modernist opus pioneered metafiction and challenged bourgeois morality in interwar France.

books.title: Atonement
books.author: Ian McEwan
books.summary: Briony Tallis’s false accusation in 1935 devastates lovers Cecilia and Robbie; decades later she seeks redemption through fiction.
books.historical_context: McEwan’s 2001 novel revisited WWII Britain and postmodern questions of narrative guilt and memory.

books.title: Berlin Alexanderplatz
books.author: Alfred Döblin
books.summary: Ex‑convict Franz Biberkopf vows honesty in Weimar Berlin’s Alexanderplatz but is dragged back into crime and despair by underworld forces.
books.historical_context: Döblin’s 1929 montage captured urban fragmentation and social unrest on the eve of Nazi ascendancy.

books.title: My Antonia
books.author: Willa Cather
books.summary: Narrator Jim Burden recalls immigrant Antonia Shimerda’s hardships and vitality on the Nebraska frontier, celebrating pioneer spirit.
books.historical_context: Cather’s 1918 novel reflected 1880s Plains settlement and shifting American agrarian ideals.

books.title: Gargantua and Pantagruel
books.author: Francois Rabelais
books.summary: Giants Gargantua and Pantagruel embark on bawdy adventures that lampoon scholastic pedantry and clerical corruption with exuberant humanism.
books.historical_context: Rabelais wrote the five books 1532‑64 amid French Renaissance debates on learning and religious reform.

books.title: A House for Mr. Biswas
books.author: V. S. Naipaul
books.summary: In colonial Trinidad, hapless journalist Mohun Biswas struggles against his domineering in‑laws to buy a house and secure personal dignity.
books.historical_context: Naipaul’s 1961 novel portrayed Indo‑Caribbean identity and post‑colonial social mobility.

books.title: The Once and Future King
books.author: T. H. White
books.summary: From young Wart’s education by wizard Merlin to the downfall of Camelot, White retells Arthurian legend with humor, psychology and pacifism.
books.historical_context: Published 1938‑58, the quartet reflected WWII experiences, questioning power and human nature through myth.

books.title: U.S.A. Trilogy
books.author: John Dos Passos
books.summary: Experimental trilogy uses newsreels, biographies, stream-of-consciousness to capture American life.
books.historical_context: Published from 1930 to 1936, the U.S.A. Trilogy pioneered modernist collage techniques in American epic fiction, intercutting fictional narratives with newsreels, biographies, and stream-of-consciousness to capture a nation in crisis.

books.title: Communist Manifesto
books.author: Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels
books.summary: Political pamphlet advocates proletarian revolution to overthrow bourgeois capitalism.
books.historical_context: Published 1848, sparked communist movements and shaped modern political thought.

books.title: The Autobiography of Malcolm X
books.author: Alex Haley
books.summary: Malcolm X's transformation from criminal to Nation of Islam minister to human rights advocate.
books.historical_context: Published in 1965 shortly after Malcolm X's assassination, his autobiography as told to Alex Haley became a defining text of the Black Power movement, tracing his transformation from street criminal to Nation of Islam minister to global human rights advocate.

books.title: All the King's Men
books.author: Robert Penn Warren
books.summary: Rise and fall of populist politician Willie Stark exposes corruption and moral compromise.
books.historical_context: Published in 1946, All the King's Men won the Pulitzer Prize for its portrait of a Southern demagogue inspired by Louisiana's Huey Long, exploring how idealism curdles into corruption and the impossibility of political innocence.

books.title: Never Let Me Go
books.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
books.summary: Clone students at boarding school gradually realize their fate as organ donors.
books.historical_context: Published in 2005, Never Let Me Go is Kazuo Ishiguro's quietly devastating dystopian meditation on mortality, love, and what makes us human, following students at an English boarding school who gradually discover their true purpose.

books.title: Bonjour Tristesse
books.author: Francoise Sagan
books.summary: Teenage Cécile manipulates her father's love life with tragic consequences on French Riviera.
books.historical_context: Published in 1954, Bonjour Tristesse made 18-year-old Francoise Sagan an overnight literary sensation, her scandalous debut about a teenager's manipulation of her father's love life defining postwar French youth rebellion and moral ambiguity.

books.title: A Confederacy of Dunces
books.author: John Kennedy Toole
books.summary: Ignatius J. Reilly, misanthropic medievalist, wreaks havoc in 1960s New Orleans.
books.historical_context: Published posthumously in 1980 after John Kennedy Toole's suicide, A Confederacy of Dunces won the Pulitzer Prize thanks to his mother's persistence in finding a publisher, becoming a beloved comic portrait of New Orleans eccentricity.

books.title: A Christmas Carol
books.author: Charles Dickens
books.summary: Miserly Ebenezer Scrooge is visited by three spirits on Christmas Eve who show him visions of past, present, and future, transforming him from a cold-hearted businessman into a generous and compassionate man.
books.historical_context: Published in 1843, A Christmas Carol was written by Dickens in just six weeks and became his most enduringly popular work, single-handedly reviving Christmas traditions and creating the template for holiday redemption stories.

books.title: Anne of Green Gables
books.author: L. M. Montgomery
books.summary: Imaginative orphan Anne Shirley transforms lives at Green Gables farm with spirit and determination.
books.historical_context: Published in 1908, Anne of Green Gables launched a beloved Canadian literary phenomenon, with L.M. Montgomery's spirited orphan heroine inspiring worldwide devotion and turning Prince Edward Island into a literary pilgrimage site.

books.title: Disgrace
books.author: J. M. Coetzee
books.summary: Disgraced professor David Lurie confronts post-apartheid violence at daughter's farm.
books.historical_context: Published in 1999, Disgrace won J.M. Coetzee his second Booker Prize for its unflinching examination of race, power, and post-apartheid South Africa through a disgraced professor's confrontation with the new realities of the Rainbow Nation.

books.title: The Murder of Roger Ackroyd
books.author: Agatha Christie
books.summary: Hercule Poirot investigates village murder with shocking narrator twist ending.
books.historical_context: Published in 1926, The Murder of Roger Ackroyd revolutionized the detective genre with its shocking narrative twist, establishing Agatha Christie as the undisputed Queen of Crime and Hercule Poirot as fiction's greatest detective.

books.title: The Road
books.author: Cormac McCarthy
books.summary: Father and son journey through post-apocalyptic wasteland toward southern coast.
books.historical_context: Published 2006, won Pulitzer, McCarthy's meditation on love amid total devastation.

books.title: East of Eden
books.author: John Steinbeck

location.name: California, Massachusetts, Salinas, San Francisco, United States

books.summary: Two families' intertwined saga parallels Cain and Abel story in Salinas Valley.
books.historical_context: Published in 1952, Steinbeck called East of Eden his magnum opus, a sweeping retelling of the Cain and Abel story set across three generations in California's Salinas Valley during America's transition from agrarian to modern society.

books.title: A Wizard of Earthsea
books.author: Ursula K. Le Guin
books.summary: Young wizard Ged must confront shadow he unleashed to restore world's balance.
books.historical_context: Published in 1968, A Wizard of Earthsea revolutionized fantasy literature with its non-white protagonist and Taoist-influenced magic system, establishing Ursula K. Le Guin as a major voice and influencing generations of fantasy writers.

books.title: The Haunting of Hill House
books.author: Shirley Jackson
books.summary: Four people investigate haunted mansion where house itself preys on Eleanor's psyche.
books.historical_context: Published 1959, defined modern psychological horror, inspired countless adaptations.

books.title: Women in Love
books.author: D. H. Lawrence
books.summary: Two sisters and their lovers navigate destructive passion in industrial England.
books.historical_context: Published 1920, banned for obscenity, Lawrence's exploration of modern sexuality.

books.title: The Life of Samuel Johnson
books.author: James Boswell
books.summary: Boswell's intimate portrait of Dr. Johnson's wit, wisdom, and eccentricities in 18th-century London society.
books.historical_context: 1791 biography revolutionizing the genre through detailed observation and dialogue.

books.title: The Lion, The Witch and the Wardrobe
books.author: C. S. Lewis
books.summary: Four children enter Narnia through wardrobe to defeat White Witch with Aslan's help.
books.historical_context: Published in 1950, The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe launched the beloved Chronicles of Narnia series, with C.S. Lewis weaving Christian allegory into a children's fantasy that has sold over 100 million copies worldwide.

books.title: Of Human Bondage
books.author: W. Somerset Maugham
books.summary: Club-footed Philip Carey struggles through medical school and destructive love obsession.
books.historical_context: Published in 1915, Of Human Bondage is W. Somerset Maugham's semi-autobiographical masterpiece chronicling Philip Carey's struggles with disability, unrequited love, and the search for meaning in Edwardian England and bohemian Paris.

books.title: The House of Mirth
books.author: Edith Wharton

location.name: New York, New York City, United States

books.summary: Lily Bart's descent from high society to poverty in rigid Gilded Age New York.
books.historical_context: Published in 1905, The House of Mirth is Edith Wharton's devastating critique of the limited options available to women in Gilded Age New York society, tracing Lily Bart's tragic descent from social grace to ruin.

books.title: Hunger
books.author: Knut Hamsun
books.summary: Starving writer in Oslo descends into delirious hallucinations while maintaining fierce pride.
books.historical_context: 1890 psychological novel pioneering stream-of-consciousness, influenced Kafka and Camus.

books.title: Persuasion
books.author: Jane Austen
books.summary: Anne Elliot gets second chance with Captain Wentworth eight years after broken engagement.
books.historical_context: Published posthumously in 1817, Persuasion is Jane Austen's most mature and emotionally complex novel, a meditation on lost love and second chances that many consider her finest achievement in its autumnal wisdom.

books.title: Democracy in America
books.author: Alexis de Tocqueville
books.summary: French aristocrat analyzes American democracy's strengths and dangers, predicting tyranny of majority.
books.historical_context: 1835-40 study remains definitive analysis of American political culture and democracy.

books.title: Zorba the Greek
books.author: Nikos Kazantzakis
books.summary: Bookish narrator learns to embrace life from exuberant Zorba on Crete.
books.historical_context: Published in 1946, Zorba the Greek celebrates Mediterranean vitality and instinctive wisdom against modern alienation, with Nikos Kazantzakis creating one of literature's most life-affirming characters in the irrepressible Alexis Zorba.

books.title: The Republic
books.author: Plato
books.summary: Socrates debates ideal state structure, philosopher-kings, justice, and cave allegory.
books.historical_context: Written around 380 BCE, The Republic is the foundation of Western political philosophy, where Plato uses Socratic dialogue to explore justice, the ideal state, and the philosopher-king, profoundly shaping two millennia of thought.

books.title: The Tempest
books.author: William Shakespeare
books.summary: Exiled Prospero uses magic on island to reclaim dukedom and reconcile with enemies.
books.historical_context: Written around 1610-11, The Tempest is widely considered Shakespeare's farewell to the stage, a magical romance about forgiveness, colonial encounter, and the transformative power of art on an enchanted island.

books.title: Nausea
books.author: Jean Paul Sartre
books.summary: Roquentin's diary chronicles existential crisis and physical revulsion at existence itself.
books.historical_context: Published 1938, foundational existentialist novel exploring being and consciousness.

books.title: The Turn of the Screw
books.author: Henry James
books.summary: Governess at Bly Manor sees ghosts threatening children—or descends into madness.
books.historical_context: Published in 1898, The Turn of the Screw is Henry James's masterfully ambiguous ghost story that epitomizes psychological horror, leaving readers to debate whether its ghosts are real or the narrator's hallucinations.

books.title: A Season in Hell
books.author: Arthur Rimbaud
books.summary: Teenage poet's hallucinatory confession of debauchery, failed love with Verlaine, and spiritual crisis.
books.historical_context: 1873 prose poem written at 19 after shooting scandal, pioneered Symbolist movement.

books.author: Roberto Bolaño
books.summary: Five interconnected parts spiral around Ciudad Juárez femicides, reclusive writer, and apocalyptic evil.
books.historical_context: 2004 posthumous magnum opus, 900 pages confronting violence at millennium's edge.

books.title: Finnegans Wake
books.author: James Joyce
books.summary: Dreamlike cyclical narrative of publican Earwicker's fall written in multilingual puns.
books.historical_context: Published in 1939, Finnegans Wake is James Joyce's final and most experimental work, written in a revolutionary dream language that fuses dozens of languages, representing the most radical experiment in the history of the English novel.

books.title: The Secret History
books.author: Donna Tartt
books.summary: Elite classics students murder friend Bunny after Dionysian rites go wrong at Vermont college.
books.historical_context: Published in 1992, The Secret History launched the dark academia genre with its reverse murder mystery set at an elite Vermont college, exploring how a group of classics students' pursuit of beauty leads to moral catastrophe.

books.title: Collected Poems of W. B. Yeats
books.author: William Butler Yeats
books.summary: From Celtic twilight through Easter Rising to mystical late works: 'The Second Coming,' 'Sailing to Byzantium.'
books.historical_context: 1923 Nobel laureate's evolution from Romanticism to Modernism, Irish cultural revival leader.

books.title: Invisible Cities
books.author: Italo Calvino
books.summary: Marco Polo describes 55 fantastical cities to Kublai Khan, all versions of Venice.
books.historical_context: Published in 1972, Invisible Cities is Italo Calvino's postmodern masterpiece in which Marco Polo describes impossible cities to Kublai Khan, blending philosophy and imagination into a meditation on memory, desire, and language.

books.title: Tropic of Cancer
books.author: Henry Miller
books.summary: Bohemian American writer's raw experiences in 1930s Paris poverty and sexual freedom.
books.historical_context: Published in 1934 in Paris and banned in the US until 1961 for obscenity, Tropic of Cancer is Henry Miller's autobiographical novel of bohemian squalor that shattered literary taboos and expanded the boundaries of what fiction could express.

books.title: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier and Clay
books.author: Michael Chabon

location.name: New York, New York City, United States

books.summary: Jewish cousins create superhero comics while escaping Nazi Prague and hiding homosexuality.
books.historical_context: Published 2000, won Pulitzer, celebrates Golden Age comics and immigrant experience.

books.title: Death of Virgil
books.author: Hermann Broch
books.summary: Virgil's final 18 hours contemplating whether to burn the Aeneid, stream-of-consciousness deathbed meditation.
books.historical_context: Published in 1945, The Death of Virgil is Hermann Broch's modernist masterpiece written during his US exile from Nazi Austria, a lyrical stream-of-consciousness meditation on art, death, and the poet's responsibility to society.

books.title: Out of Africa
books.author: Isak Dinesen

location.name: Africa, Kenya, Southern Africa, Sub-Saharan Africa

books.summary: Danish baroness's lyrical memoir of running coffee farm in colonial Kenya.
books.historical_context: Published in 1937, Out of Africa is Isak Dinesen's lyrical memoir of running a coffee plantation in Kenya, romanticizing colonial Africa and the landscape of the Ngong Hills in prose of exceptional beauty.

books.title: The Jungle
books.author: Upton Sinclair
books.summary: Lithuanian immigrant Jurgis faces brutal Chicago meatpacking industry exploitation.
books.historical_context: Published in 1906, The Jungle exposed such horrific conditions in Chicago's meatpacking industry that it led directly to the passage of the Pure Food and Drug Act and the Meat Inspection Act.

books.title: The Stand
books.author: Stephen King

location.name: Boulder, Colorado, Las Vegas, United States

books.summary: Superflu survivors choose between Mother Abagail's good and Randall Flagg's evil.
books.historical_context: Published in 1978, The Stand is Stephen King's apocalyptic epic of good versus evil following a devastating plague, reflecting Cold War anxieties about biological weapons and societal collapse.

books.title: Malone Dies
books.author: Samuel Beckett
books.summary: Bedridden Malone invents stories while dying alone, cataloging possessions and memories in disintegrating prose.
books.historical_context: 1951 second novel of Beckett's trilogy, written in French, exploring consciousness's end.

books.title: The World According to Garp
books.author: John Irving
books.summary: Writer T.S. Garp navigates life with feminist mother Jenny Fields amid violence and chaos.
books.historical_context: Published in 1978, The World According to Garp is John Irving's breakthrough tragicomedy about a writer's life in modern America, blending absurdist humor with violence and grief in a novel that became a major bestseller and cultural touchstone.

books.title: The Moviegoer
books.author: Walker Percy
books.summary: Stockbroker Binx Bolling searches for meaning through movies and affairs in New Orleans during Mardi Gras.
books.historical_context: 1962 National Book Award winner exploring existential malaise in prosperous America.

books.title: The Forsyte Saga
books.author: John Galsworthy
books.summary: Three generations of propertied Forsytes struggle between passion and possession in Victorian-Edwardian England.
books.historical_context: Published from 1906 to 1921, The Forsyte Saga earned John Galsworthy the 1932 Nobel Prize for its sweeping chronicle of British upper-middle-class decline, exposing the conflict between property and passion in Edwardian England.

books.title: The Complete Stories of Franz Kafka
books.author: Franz Kafka
books.summary: Collected stories featuring bureaucratic nightmares, metamorphoses, and existential parables of modern alienation.
books.historical_context: Posthumous collections from 1920s Prague, defining 20th-century anxieties as 'Kafkaesque.'

books.title: The Tartar Steppe
books.author: Dino Buzzati
books.summary: Officer Drogo wastes entire life at remote fortress waiting for enemy attack that never comes.
books.historical_context: 1940 Italian existential novel about futile waiting, influenced by Kafka and desert warfare.

books.title: Uncle Tom's Cabin
books.author: Harriet Beecher Stowe

location.name: Kentucky, Louisiana, Ohio, Southern US, United States

books.summary: Enslaved Uncle Tom's faith endures through multiple masters' cruelty in antebellum South.
books.historical_context: Published in 1852, Uncle Tom's Cabin galvanized the abolitionist movement with its powerful indictment of slavery, selling 300,000 copies in its first year and helping precipitate the Civil War — Lincoln allegedly told Stowe she started the war.

books.title: The Gulag Archipelago
books.author: Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn
books.summary: Exposé of Soviet forced labor camps based on testimonies and author's imprisonment.
books.historical_context: Published in 1973 in the West after being smuggled from the Soviet Union, The Gulag Archipelago exposed the vast system of Soviet labor camps, earning Solzhenitsyn the Nobel Prize and helping delegitimize Communist ideology worldwide.

books.title: The Godfather
books.author: Mario Puzo
books.summary: Vito Corleone's crime family navigates power struggles as son Michael becomes new Don.
books.historical_context: Published in 1969, The Godfather defined the mafia genre in popular culture and became Mario Puzo's breakthrough novel, later adapted by Coppola into one of cinema's greatest achievements.

books.title: Dangerous Liaison
books.author: Pierre Choderlos de Laclos
books.summary: Aristocrats Valmont and Merteuil manipulate lovers through letters, destroying lives for sport.
books.historical_context: Published in 1782, Dangerous Liaisons is an epistolary masterpiece exposing the moral depravity of pre-Revolutionary French aristocratic society through the sexual games of its manipulative protagonists, presaging the Revolution's destruction of that world.

books.title: Siddhartha
books.author: Hermann Hesse
books.summary: Young Brahmin's spiritual journey from asceticism to enlightenment by river.
books.historical_context: Published in 1922, Siddhartha is Hermann Hesse's lyrical exploration of Buddhist philosophy and the quest for self-discovery in ancient India, becoming a spiritual touchstone for the 1960s counterculture and selling millions worldwide.

books.title: Medea
books.author: Euripides
books.summary: Abandoned Medea murders her children to revenge Jason's betrayal for new bride.
books.historical_context: Written in 431 BCE by Euripides, Medea is a searing Greek tragedy exploring female rage and vengeance that shocked Athenian audiences by making a foreign woman its complex, sympathetic antihero who defies patriarchal power.

books.title: Oblomov
books.author: Ivan Goncharov
books.summary: Lazy nobleman Oblomov refuses to leave bed or estate, epitomizing Russian aristocratic paralysis.
books.historical_context: 1859 satire coined 'Oblomovism' for Russian gentry's lethargy before emancipation.

books.title: The Double Helix
books.author: James D. Watson
books.summary: Watson's candid account of discovering DNA structure with Crick, revealing scientific competition and personalities.
books.historical_context: Published in 1968, The Double Helix is James Watson's controversial memoir revealing DNA's discovery as a fiercely competitive human endeavor rather than noble pursuit, exposing how Rosalind Franklin's work was appropriated.

books.title: The Adventures of Augie March
books.author: Saul Bellow
books.summary: Chicago hustler Augie March refuses to be tied down, pursuing adventures from Depression to Mexico.
books.historical_context: Published in 1953, The Adventures of Augie March won the National Book Award and launched the Jewish-American literary renaissance, with Saul Bellow's exuberant, picaresque Chicago hero declaring 'I am an American, Chicago born.'

books.title: Darkness at Noon
books.author: Arthur Koestler
books.summary: Old Bolshevik Rubashov imprisoned, tortured into confessing imaginary crimes during Stalin's purges.
books.historical_context: Published in 1940, Darkness at Noon is Arthur Koestler's devastating anti-totalitarian novel based on the Moscow show trials, depicting an old Bolshevik's interrogation and confession, profoundly influencing Orwell and Cold War intellectuals.

books.title: The Chronicles of Narnia
books.author: C. S. Lewis
books.summary: Seven fantasy novels where children discover magical Narnia through wardrobe and other portals.
books.historical_context: Published from 1950 to 1956, The Chronicles of Narnia is C.S. Lewis's beloved seven-volume fantasy series weaving Christian allegory into imaginative adventure, selling over 100 million copies and inspiring generations of readers.

books.title: The Power and the Glory
books.author: Graham Greene
books.summary: Whisky priest flees persecution in 1930s Mexico while struggling with faith and sin.
books.historical_context: Published 1940, Greene's Catholic novel set during Mexican anti-clerical purges.

books.title: The Shining
books.author: Stephen King
books.summary: Jack Torrance's winter caretaking at Overlook Hotel spirals into supernatural madness.
books.historical_context: Published in 1977, The Shining was Stephen King's third novel and established him as the master of modern horror, drawing on his own struggles with alcoholism to create the iconic haunted hotel of the Overlook.

books.title: The Radetzky March
books.author: Joseph Roth
books.summary: Three generations of Trotta family mirror Habsburg Empire's decline from heroism to dissolution.
books.historical_context: Published in 1932, The Radetzky March is Joseph Roth's elegiac masterpiece about the decline and fall of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, written in exile as Roth witnessed the rise of Nazism destroying his world.

books.title: Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret
books.author: Judy Blume

location.name: Farbrook, New Jersey, New York, United States

books.summary: Eleven-year-old Margaret navigates puberty, religion, and friendship in new suburb.
books.historical_context: Published in 1970, Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret was a groundbreaking young adult novel that frankly addressed female adolescence, puberty, and religious questioning, becoming one of the most beloved and frequently banned children's books.

books.title: Life, a User's Manual
books.author: Georges Perec
books.summary: 99 chapters explore Paris apartment building's residents, centered on puzzle-maker's revenge scheme.
books.historical_context: 1978 Oulipo masterwork using mathematical constraints to create narrative labyrinth.

books.title: Nostromo
books.author: Joseph Conrad
books.summary: Silver mine corrupts fictional South American republic during revolution and civil war.
books.historical_context: Published in 1904, Nostromo is Joseph Conrad's political masterpiece set in a fictional South American republic, exploring how imperialism, greed, and idealism collide, considered by many critics his greatest achievement.

books.title: Babbitt
books.author: Sinclair Lewis
books.summary: Real estate agent George Babbitt rebels against middle-class conformity in Midwest city.
books.historical_context: Published 1922, satirized American business culture, added 'Babbitt' to language.

books.title: Look Homeward, Angel
books.author: Thomas Wolfe
books.summary: Eugene Gant escapes suffocating Southern family and town to pursue artistic destiny at university.
books.historical_context: 1929 autobiographical debut scandalized Asheville, launched Wolfe's torrential style.

books.title: Life and Fate
books.author: Vasily Grossman
books.summary: Stalingrad battle interweaves Soviet families' fates under Stalin and Hitler's tyrannies.
books.historical_context: Written in the 1950s but suppressed by Soviet authorities until 1980 publication in the West, Life and Fate is Vasily Grossman's epic of Stalingrad often compared to War and Peace, a devastating indictment of totalitarianism.

books.title: Independent People
books.author: Halldor Laxness
books.summary: Stubborn sheep farmer Bjartur's fierce independence destroys family in harsh Icelandic countryside.
books.historical_context: Published from 1934 to 1935, Independent People won Halldor Laxness the 1955 Nobel Prize as Iceland's great 20th-century novel, depicting a stubborn sheep farmer's fierce independence against the harsh Icelandic landscape.

books.title: Thus Spake Zarathustra
